query: Pulsatile varicose veins in lower limbs

retrieved_docs.title: Venous Insufficiency -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) commonly present initially with a combination of dependent pitting edema, leg discomfort, fatigue, and itching. Although there can be variations in presentation among patients, certain features are more prevalent, including telangiectasias, reticular veins, varicose veins, pain, cramping, itching, prickling, and throbbing sensation. Patients may describe symptoms that improve with rest and leg elevation and with no association with exercise. This latter feature can be used to distinguish venous from arterial claudication. As their disease progresses, varicose veins, tenderness, refractory edema, and skin changes can be noted. (see Image. Chronic Venous Insufficiency) Patients with advanced disease will present with a severe blanched skin lesion, dermal atrophy, hyperpigmentation, dilated venous capillaries, and ulcer formation, most commonly overlying the medial malleolus. A thorough history should note any hypercoagulable condition, oral contraceptive use, previous DVT or intervention, level of physical activity, and occupation. The patient's presentation should carefully be distinguished from other pathologies with similar symptoms: diabetic ulcers, ischemic ulcers, and dermatologic conditions, including cancer. [4] [5] [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Infrainguinal Occlusive Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Leriche syndrome: Aortoiliac occlusive disease, which presents as a triad of buttock claudication, impotence, lower limb extremity wasting. Buerger disease: most common in young male smokers, may present with gangrenous digits, pain, and impalpable pulses. Vasculitis Deep venous thrombosis: can present with calf pain and swelling. Risk factors may be present. Osteoarthritis Popliteal compartment syndrome Neurogenic claudication: Lumbar stenosis, herniated disc; this can be differentiated as it may not be reproducible with exertion and may not be present at all times.  Examination findings such as skin temperature, delayed capillary refill, and absence of pulses will be absent.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Veins -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Venous reflux disease is a common condition that results in a considerable lifestyle impact due to the symptoms of leg pain and swelling, and that has the potential to cause more significant morbidity by eventual venous ulcer formation. Treatment with endovenous ablation with a period of compression therapy is usually effective in reducing the associated symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Venous Leg Ulcer -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Color-flow duplex ultrasound is another inexpensive, non-invasive, and highly informative diagnostic test useful for superficial vein assessment. [34] [44] The technique can identify thrombi presence and valve incompetence, declared when reflux time exceeds 0.5 seconds. [45] Computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging are preferred for deeper vessels since they are often harder or impossible to evaluate with ultrasound. Phlebography, plethysmography, and phlebodynamometry are less used due to inferior accuracy and associated risks. [46] [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Venous Insufficiency -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam should involve a detailed assessment of any ulcers, distal pulses, and neuropathy in the upright and supine positions. The Trendelenburg test may help differentiate between the CVI caused by the superficial vein valves and the deep system. The patient's leg is elevated to perform the test, and all the venous blood is emptied. The surgeon then compresses the groin firmly to occlude the greater saphenous vein junction and asks the patient to stand up. If the leg does not fill up with venous blood, this indicates that incompetent valves in the superficial veins cause CVI. If the leg fills with venous blood, the valves connecting the superficial veins to the deep ones are incompetent. The treatment is limited to compression stockings if the deep system valves are involved. [4] [5] [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb, Foot Veins -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose veins are a common pathology seen in the foot and ankle veins. They are ectatic, tortuous vessels of the superficial venous system that are at least 3 mm in size and arise from the failure of venous valves to close properly to allow the backward flow of blood. [14] The 2 main superficial leg veins (the great saphenous and small saphenous veins) and their branches are the most common sites of varicose veins. [14] Patients with varicose veins may present with no symptoms other than the enlarged vein, but some may experience pain, burning, itching, or leg swelling. These varicose veins should be taken seriously because they present a cosmetic problem and increase the risk of developing superficial vein thrombosis and venous thromboembolic disease. [14] The treatment of varicose veins includes conservative and surgical treatments. Conservative treatment of varicose veins includes elastic compression stockings to control swelling and weight loss and topical steroid cream to control inflammation. Surgical treatment includes varicose vein stripping, phlebectomy, endovenous thermoablation, radiofrequency ablation, and foam sclerotherapy. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Vascular and Lymphatic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Venous Sonography
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral veins are normally low velocity and phasic due to respiratory variation. [14] Valsalva maneuver will cause a reversal of flow within the lower extremity veins. Calculation of the time of flow reversal in venous vessels helps determine venous insufficiency.  For instance, venous reflux lasting more than 1 sec in the deep veins, or 0.5 sec at any other level, is clinically significant for venous insufficiency (varicose veins). Squeezing the patient’s calf or having them contract their surrounding lower extremity muscles causes a phenomenon known as augmentation of flow. Lack of augmentation indicates stenosis, usually due to venous thrombosis. Loss of respiratory phasicity indicates proximal stenosis. The deep venous system includes the common femoral, femoral vein, popliteal veins, and its tributaries. The superficial venous system includes the great and small saphenous veins and the medial marginal vein.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Vascular Peripheral Vein Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Only color and spectral Doppler are used at the proximal common femoral and popliteal veins since satisfactory compression is technically infeasible in these areas (see Image. Color Doppler Depicting Loss of Phasicity in the Common Femoral Vein). At the common femoral vein, spectral Doppler evaluates for cardiac pulsatility and respiratory phasicity, which indirectly suggest patency between the common femoral vein and the heart as an occlusive disease would prevent the expected propagation of these cyclical changes (see Image. Normal Respiratory Phasicity). Similarly, augmentation of the calf veins by manual compression should manifest as a sharp peak in the popliteal waveform, indicating at least partial patency of the deep venous structures in the calf. [32] This maneuver is particularly helpful as the infra-popliteal veins are often problematic to delineate due to overlying edema or trophic skin changes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Veins -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Contrast venography is rarely done today. Pulsed-wave Doppler images of varicose veins in axial projection will demonstrate retrograde blood flow after the release of manual calf compression. In the varicose vein with reflux, the reflux can be easily quantified with an ultrasound probe angled steeply upward while imaging in axial projection. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Venous Leg Ulcer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Venous leg ulcers (VLUs) are late indicators of chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) and venous hypertension. [1] [2] [3] [4] Calf muscle contraction and intraluminal valves promote prograde flow while preventing blood reflux in normal conditions. [5] However, when retrograde flow, obstruction, or both exist, chronic venous hypertension is responsible for the dermatologic and vascular complications that form a VLU (See Image . Venous Leg Ulcer). [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Venous Reconstruction -- Indications -- Presenting Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: CVO presents with venous claudication consistent with exercise-induced limb swelling and pain relieved with leg elevation and rest. [6] Symptoms such as edema, varicosity, lipodermatosclerosis, eczema, and ulceration resemble those seen in CVI. [11] However, CVO presents with more pronounced claudication, edema, and relief with limb elevation or rest tends to be less effective. [12] CVO may lead to compromised limb viability depending on the extent of collateral circulation. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Venous Insufficiency(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple processes contribute to the development of venous disease wherein venous pressure is elevated and blood return is impeded, such as valvular incompetence, thrombotic or non-thrombotic venous blockage, or extrinsic compression of the veins. These variables are worsened by dysfunctional muscular pumps, particularly in the calf muscles. These trajectories result in global or localized venous hypertension, especially when standing or walking. Persistent venous hypertension can result in hyperpigmentation of the skin, lipodermatosclerosis of the subcutaneous tissue, and, eventually, ulceration.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Veins -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Initial diagnosis is by clinical evaluation of swelling, discoloration, and skin ulcerations. Symptoms associated with varicose veins include: Leg heaviness Itching Cramps Mild tenderness Skin discoloration Exercise intolerance Leg fatigue [22] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb, Foot Veins -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The foot veins are susceptible to several pathologies, including corona phelbectatica, varicose veins, deep vein thrombosis, and venous ulcers. [1] Corona phelbectatica is the presence of abnormally visible cutaneous blood vessels at the ankle with 4 distinct components: "venous cups," blue and red telangiectasias, and capillary "stasis spots." [1] [13] The veins in corona phelbectatica are frequently referred to as "ankle spider veins." They are a clear sign of serious chronic venous insufficiency that requires medical attention because of their potential to result in more serious conditions, including blood clots or leg ulcers. [1] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Venous Insufficiency(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of the chronic venous disease remains elusive. It appears to have a hereditary component since genetic disorders like Klippel-Trenaunay and Parkes-Weber are known to cause CVD. [4] The risk factors associated with CVD are as follows: Age Sex Overweight Use of oral contraceptives Tobacco use Pregnancy Family history of varicose veins A history of deep vein thrombosis History of thrombophlebitis History of the leg injury Prolonged standing or sitting

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Stripping of lower limb varicose veins is classified as a vascular surgical procedure on the lower limb, representing a specific operative technique used to treat varicose veins by removing or ligating the diseased superficial veins. This procedure falls within the broader category of lower‑limb vascular surgeries, which encompass various interventions aimed at restoring normal venous and arterial function in the legs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose veins of the lower extremity are commonly linked to the development of قَرحَة (skin ulcer), indicating that ulcerative lesions represent a typical morphological manifestation in patients with these venous disorders. This association highlights the importance of monitoring for ulcer formation when managing lower‑extremity varicose veins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a surgical technique that is performed directly on veins, specifically targeting the abnormal, dilated vessels known as varicose veins. The procedure involves ligating (tying off) the affected vein to prevent blood flow and then stripping (removing) the vein segment, thereby alleviating symptoms such as pain, swelling, and cosmetic concerns. By acting directly on the vein, this intervention addresses the underlying venous insufficiency that leads to varicosities and contributes to improved lower‑extremity circulation and patient quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2379
retrieved_docs.content: Stripping of lower limb varicose veins is classified as a vascular surgical procedure on the lower limb and represents a specific form of varicose vein stripping; lower limb, indicating that it is a targeted operative technique used to treat varicose veins affecting the lower extremities.

Varicose veins of lower extremity is itself a specific manifestation within تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية, and it is associated with the morphology of قَرحَة (ulcer). This relationship highlights that ulcer formation is a common morphological complication of varicose veins in the lower extremities, emphasizing the importance of recognizing ulcer risk when diagnosing and managing patients with lower‑extremity varicose veins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2651
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping directly targets the vein (vény), reflecting that this surgical procedure is performed on the vascular structures themselves. The vein (vény) also has an indirect procedural association with blood flow (إراقة الدم), underscoring its essential role in circulating blood throughout the body. This relationship highlights how the treatment of varicose veins is intrinsically linked to both the anatomical site of the veins and their functional involvement in blood movement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2648
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose veins of lower extremity, also referred to by the Arabic term تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية, represent a vascular condition in which superficial veins of the legs become dilated and tortuous. This condition is closely linked to the development of ulcers, described by the Arabic word قَرحَة, indicating that ulcer formation is a common morphological complication of varicose veins of lower extremity. Understanding that varicose veins of lower extremity are a manifestation of تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية and that they may lead to قَرحَة is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: limb and is the finding site for varicose veins of lower extremity. Varicose veins of lower extremity are associated morphologically with قَرحَة (ulceration) and are conceptually nested under تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية (vein sagging in the lower limbs), the parent term describing the clinical condition of lower‑extremity varicosities. Together, these relationships delineate a coherent framework in which arterial embolectomy/endarterectomy and venous varicose‑vein ligation/stripping are organized as specialized incisional vascular surgeries on the lower limbs, linked by common procedural themes such as vessel incision, blood drainage, and the anatomical focus on lower‑extremity arteries and veins. Understanding this hierarchy aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical techniques, anticipating related outcomes such as ulcer formation, and recognizing the interconnected nature of arterial and venous interventions in lower‑limb vascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1165
retrieved_docs.content: Stripping of lower limb varicose veins and varicose vein ligation and stripping are specific vascular surgical procedures performed on the lower limb, both classified under the broader category of varicose vein stripping; lower limb. These procedures target the structure of the vein of the lower extremity, which serves as the direct site of intervention. Recognizing that both techniques belong to the same procedural class aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical approaches for treating varicose veins of the lower extremities.
